text:
Sodium-ion batteries (SIBs) have been regarded as one of the promising energy storage devices for the future sustainable electric power system. However, the SIBs cathodes are currently limited by low energy due to huge stress caused by the structural expansion and shrinkage during the (de)sodiation process. Here, we report a P2-layered oxide cathode, $\text{Na}_{0.7}\text{Li}_{0.03}[\text{Mg}_{0.15}\text{Li}_{0.07}\text{Mn}_{0.75}]\text{O}_2$ (NMLMO), by adopting lithium dual-site substitution strategy where Li ions occupy both TM and AM layers, and thus reduce the intrinsic stress along with enhancing the ionic conductivity in the framework, and thereby leading to high discharge capacity, good stability and high round trip efficiency.

Combining first principle modeling and experiment studies, we demonstrate that Li adsorbed on the TM sites generates Na–O–Li electronic configuration and thus activates and enhances oxygen anionic redox capacity. In addition, the Li in the AM layers acts as LiO6 prismatic pillars which restricts the structural changes, such as P2-OP4 and P2-P’2, and thereby retains high reversible discharge capacity with superior cyclability. The NMLMO presents a high reversible discharge capacity of 266 mAh g-1 with a “nearly zero-strain” profile (less than 1.2% volumetric expansion) over a wide operation window of 1.5–4.6 V. We confirmed that the dual-site substitution approach proposed in this study can provide an efficient solution to the “devil’s dilemma” in LIBs by demonstrating the possibility of achieving high reversible discharge capacity along with maintaining stable layered structure.
